message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>the aim of this study was to test Rutherford Health (RH) staff for the presence of SARS CoV-2 antibodies to reduce the risk of infection to cancer patients.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Between 14 and 24 April 2020 we tested 161 staff at four locations: our cancer centres in Reading - Berkshire, Newport - S Wales, Liverpool - Merseyside, and Bedlington in Northumberland.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Testing was available to all staff who were on site at the four locations named above at the time the study was carried out. 161 staff (80 men, 81 women) gave voluntary consent to have the tests and all testing gave rise to valid results.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Interventions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We used the South Korean test for antibodies to SARS CoV-2: Sugentech SGTi-flex COVID-19 IgM/IgG
                    <jats:sup>1</jats:sup>
                    . For each test, blood was collected and added to the sample well of the test cassette and buffer solution added. The test result was legible after 15 minutes. Outcome measures: The number of tests positive for the presence of antibodies was the primary outcome measure. The ratio of tests positive for the presence of IgM antibodies versus IgG antibodies was the secondary outcome measure.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Between 14 and 24 April 2020, 161 staff (age m = 43) were tested at four Rutherford Cancer Care centres that offer proton beam therapy, radiotherapy and chemotherapy. Out of 161, 12 samples (7.50%) tested positive of which 7 samples (4.35%) detected IgM only, 2 samples (1.24%) detected IgG only and 3 samples (1.86%) detected both IgM and IgG.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The low seroconversion rate in the sample population limits the current utility of the test as a way of reducing risk to vulnerable patient populations but longitudinal retesting will provide further data.</jats:p>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:title>Strengths and limitations of the study</jats:title>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>This is the first UK study on SARS CoV-2 antibody testing using the Sugentech SGTi-flex COVID-19 IgM/IgG in the workplace;</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>This is the first UK study testing a population of cancer centre staff for SARS CoV-2 antibodies;</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>
                        This study builds on similar studies in other countries
                        <jats:sup>3-4</jats:sup>
                        , albeit with a smaller sample;
                      </jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Data on previous clinical symptoms is not included. We are in the process of revising consent paperwork for the test to include a question about previous symptoms like temperature, dry/persistent cough;</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Patient data is not included. We will consider testing patients once the pilot test with staff has reached conclusions about the efficacy and value of the test.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: Serological prevalence of antibodies to SARS CoV-2 amongst cancer centre staff

reference.unstructured: Bendavid E , Mulaney B , Sood N , Shah S , Ling E , Bromley-Dulfano R , et al. COVID-19 Antibody Seroprevalence in Santa Clara County, California. medRxiv [Internet]. 2020 Jan 1;2020.04.14.20062463. Available from: http://medrxiv.org/content/earlv/2020/04/17/2020.04.14.20062463.abstract
